It should be noted that Japan imports over 90% of oil from the Middle East and the government also released some of its oil reserves . To put things in perspective, posted below is an excerpt from the Kyodo News report. Some parts in boldface… A tanker carrying U.S. oil arrived in Japan on Sunday, marking the first such shipment from the United States since the Iran war began in late February. According to Cosmo Energy Holdings Co. that procured the crude oil from the United States, the tanker, which arrived in Tokyo Bay, transported 145,000 kiloliters, equivalent to half a day of domestic consumption. The U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran has led to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a key artery for global energy shipments. Japan relies on the Middle East for more than 90 percent of its crude oil imports, most of which pass through the strait. The tanker departed Texas on March 22 and transited the Panama Canal , which can accommodate smaller vessels. The Japanese government and oil wholesalers have been trying to secure oil from alternative routes to bypass the Strait of Hormuz. Western media employs double standards; when language becomes a form of reality. In the current international system, the media is not merely a conduit for news but a powerful mechanism for defining and constructing reality. What an average reader or viewer considers “truth” is often shaped through the media's selection, language, and framing. Consequently, the concepts of “double standards” and “media framing” are central to media studies. These concepts demonstrate how the media presents the same events with different political, ethical, and emotional colors. Numerous researchers in media studies believe that the language of the media is not neutral. In 2025, researchers at the University of Malaysia, in their contemporary paper “Media Framing and the Delegitimization of Palestinian Resistance,” confirmed this, noting how media outlets use language to distort perceptions. They specifically address how media framing influences perspectives on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. This discrepancy can be seen in how Western media covers the conflicts in Ukraine and Gaza, highlighting the stark differences in reporting and focus. The language, terminology, and emotional weight given to each situation differ significantly, showcasing a selective approach to portraying events and influencing public opinion. The language used to describe the casualties of the conflict between Israel and Hamas is carefully chosen. Israeli casualties are frequently named and presented with personal stories, fostering empathy and a sense of connection. Conversely, Palestinian casualties are often reduced to numbers, diminishing the sense of individual loss and humanity. This selective approach to humanization extends to broader narratives surrounding both conflicts. Ukraine receives extensive live coverage, in-depth analysis, and significant political response, while coverage of Gaza is often limited and focuses primarily on statistics. Similarly, the intensity of international sanctions and political pressure differs significantly, highlighting a pattern of “selective enforcement.” The roots of this double standard in Western media are deeply intertwined with historical and cultural contexts. As argued by Palestinian-American scholar Edward Said in his seminal work "Orientalism," the West portrays the East as “other,” often associating it with irrationality, violence, and backwardness. This kind of historical bias manifests in the language used by the media, leading to the portrayal of certain societies in a negative light. Ultimately, the language isn't merely a tool for communication but a subtle yet powerful instrument of influence, shaping perceptions and reinforcing existing power structures. The Role of Mohammed bin Zayed and Foreign Policy When current President Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan was still ‘Crown Prince’, the UAE reformed the military, making it one of the best-trained and equipped in the region. The intervention in the war in Yemen (which began in 2015) marked a turning point, demonstrating a willingness to assert itself as a strategic actor in the Persian Gulf and the Middle East. The Rise to Power of Mohammed bin Zayed MBZ holds the position of Supreme Commander of the UAE Armed Forces. In 2004 he was appointed Crown Prince, and his subsequent appointment as President the day after his father’s death was predictable, as a demonstration of unity and political continuity in the country. Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan had, in reality, been de facto ruler since 2014, the year his father suffered a stroke, and in 2019 the New York Times had already bestowed upon him the title of the world’s most powerful Arab leader. The Expansion of Emirati Influence In recent years, the UAE has deployed various means – diplomatic, military, and economic – to expand its influence. # Yemen : It has been seven months since the Huthi de facto authorities arrested human rights lawyer Abdul Majid Sabra for peacefully exercising his right to freedom of expression. We reiterate our call to the Huthis for Sabra’s immediate and unconditional release. Sabra has been arbitrarily detained in Yemen since 25 September 2025, when he was arrested at his office in Sana’a by Huthi armed forces and men in civilian clothing. They presented an arrest warrant and said his arrest was due to his social media posts marking the anniversary of Yemen’s 26th September Revolution. In December 2025, Sabra was held in solitary confinement for more than two weeks. He has gone on intermittent hunger strike to protest his treatment. Seven months after his arrest, he remains arbitrarily detained without formal charges. The Huthis must immediately release him and all those who remain in arbitrary detention. This includes dozens of UN and civil society staff that have been arrested, arbitrarily detained and forcibly disappeared during widespread crackdowns in 2024 and 2025. Pending Sabra’s release, the Huthi de facto authorities must ensure he is protected from torture and other ill-treatment and that he has access to legal counsel and regular contact with his family.
